services.name: RDS
services.description: Set up, operate, and scale relational databases in the cloud. Supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Aurora.
keyPoints: Engines: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, Aurora
Multi-AZ: synchronous standby replica for high availability (failover)
Read Replicas: asynchronous copies for read scaling (up to 15 for Aurora)
Automated backups: daily snapshots + transaction logs (point-in-time recovery)
Storage Auto Scaling: automatically increases storage when threshold reached
examTips: Multi-AZ is for HIGH AVAILABILITY (automatic failover), NOT performance
Read Replicas are for READ SCALING and can be in different regions
Multi-AZ failover: DNS name stays the same, points to new primary
RDS runs in a VPC — use security groups to control access
Encryption at rest must be enabled at creation (cannot encrypt existing DB)

quiz.q: What is the primary purpose of RDS Multi-AZ deployment?
quiz.explanation: Multi-AZ provides high availability with automatic failover. The standby is synchronous but NOT used for reads. Use Read Replicas for read scaling.

quiz.q: Can you encrypt an existing unencrypted RDS instance?
options: Yes, but requires a snapshot, copy with encryption, and restore
quiz.explanation: You cannot directly encrypt an existing RDS instance. You must create an encrypted snapshot copy, then restore a new encrypted instance from it.

services.name: Aurora
services.description: MySQL and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database with up to 5x throughput of MySQL and 3x of PostgreSQL, at 1/10th the cost of commercial databases.
keyPoints: Storage: auto-scales from 10 GB to 128 TB in 10 GB increments
6 copies of data across 3 AZs (self-healing, continuous S3 backup)
Up to 15 read replicas with sub-10ms replica lag
Aurora Serverless v2: auto-scales capacity based on demand
Global Database: cross-region replication with <1 second lag
examTips: Aurora Serverless v2: scales in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU), great for variable workloads
Aurora Global Database: 1-second cross-region replication, <1-minute failover
Aurora is NOT cheaper than RDS for small workloads — it's for scale
Writer endpoint (primary) + Reader endpoint (load-balanced across replicas)
Backtrack: rewind DB to a point in time without restore (MySQL-compatible only)

quiz.q: How many copies of your data does Aurora maintain?
quiz.explanation: Aurora stores 6 copies of your data across 3 Availability Zones. It can tolerate losing 2 copies without affecting writes, and 3 without affecting reads.

services.name: DynamoDB
services.description: Fully managed NoSQL database delivering single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. Supports key-value and document data models.
keyPoints: Single-digit millisecond latency at any scale
Capacity modes: On-Demand (pay per request) or Provisioned (RCU/WCU)
Global Tables: multi-region, multi-active replication
DynamoDB Streams: ordered log of item changes (triggers Lambda)
DAX: in-memory cache for microsecond read latency
examTips: Partition key design is critical — hot partitions kill performance
WCU: 1 write/sec for items up to 1 KB. RCU: 1 strongly consistent read/sec for items up to 4 KB
Eventually consistent reads use half the RCUs of strongly consistent
Global Tables require DynamoDB Streams enabled
TTL: automatically delete expired items at no cost

quiz.q: How many RCUs does one strongly consistent read of a 4 KB item consume?
quiz.explanation: One RCU = one strongly consistent read per second for items up to 4 KB. Eventually consistent reads use 0.5 RCU.

options: Microsecond read latency via in-memory caching
quiz.explanation: DAX is an in-memory cache that sits in front of DynamoDB, reducing read latency from milliseconds to microseconds.

services.name: ElastiCache
services.description: Fully managed in-memory data store compatible with Redis or Memcached. Sub-millisecond latency for caching, session management, and real-time analytics.
keyPoints: Engines: Redis (feature-rich) and Memcached (simple, multi-threaded)
Redis: persistence, replication, pub/sub, Lua scripting, sorted sets
Memcached: multi-threaded, simple key-value, no persistence
Cluster mode: partition data across multiple shards
Redis Global Datastore: cross-region replication
examTips: Redis vs Memcached: Redis for complex data types, persistence, replication. Memcached for simple caching with multi-threading
ElastiCache is VPC-only — not publicly accessible by default
Caching strategies: Lazy Loading (cache on miss), Write-Through (cache on write)
Redis AUTH + encryption in-transit for security
Use for session state to make stateless application tiers

quiz.q: Which ElastiCache engine supports data persistence and replication?
quiz.explanation: Redis supports persistence (RDB/AOF), replication, and advanced features. Memcached is purely in-memory with no persistence.

services.name: Redshift
services.description: Fast, scalable data warehouse using columnar storage and massively parallel query execution. Analyze structured and semi-structured data across data warehouse and data lake.
keyPoints: Columnar storage: optimized for analytics queries on large datasets
Massively Parallel Processing (MPP): distributes queries across nodes
Redshift Spectrum: query S3 data directly without loading
Concurrency Scaling: handles burst read traffic automatically
AQUA (Advanced Query Accelerator): hardware-accelerated cache
examTips: Redshift is NOT for OLTP — use RDS/Aurora for transactional workloads
Redshift Serverless: auto-scales capacity, pay per query
Data loaded via COPY command (from S3) is much faster than INSERT
Distribution styles: KEY, EVEN, ALL — affects query join performance
Enhanced VPC Routing: forces all COPY/UNLOAD through VPC

quiz.q: What type of storage does Redshift use for optimal analytics performance?
quiz.explanation: Redshift uses columnar storage, which is optimal for analytics because queries typically scan specific columns across many rows rather than full rows.

services.name: DocumentDB
services.description: Fast, scalable, highly available document database compatible with MongoDB workloads. Managed service with automatic scaling storage.
keyPoints: MongoDB 3.6/4.0/5.0 compatible API
Storage auto-scales up to 128 TB in 10 GB increments
Up to 15 read replicas across 3 AZs
Continuous backup to S3 with point-in-time recovery
Fully managed: patching, backup, recovery automated
examTips: Not 100% MongoDB compatible — test your workload before migrating
Storage architecture similar to Aurora (distributed, fault-tolerant)
Use DMS (Database Migration Service) to migrate from MongoDB
DocumentDB is VPC-only, not publicly accessible
Global Clusters for cross-region disaster recovery

quiz.q: Which database API is Amazon DocumentDB compatible with?
quiz.explanation: Amazon DocumentDB is compatible with MongoDB, allowing you to use existing MongoDB drivers and tools with a fully managed AWS service.
